perturbation.original_text: E. After the closing of the transaction contemplated in the Purchase Agreement, Connors, Petmecky, Gundry, and Horton desire to continue to engage in the Business, subject, however, to the terms, conditions, and limitations set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: E. After the closing of the transaction contemplated in the Asset Transfer Agreement, Connors, Petmecky, Gundry, and Horton desire to continue to engage in the Business, subject, however, to the terms, conditions, and limitations set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Purchase Agreement' to 'Asset Transfer Agreement'. This creates confusion because the contract consistently refers to the 'Purchase Agreement'. Section B defines the 'Purchase Agreement,' and section 3 refers to the 'Asset Purchase Agreement to be executed in conjunction with this Agreement'. The altered term conflicts with these references, creating uncertainty about the agreement's validity and scope.
perturbation.contradicted_text: B. Pursuant to the Asset Purchase Agreement of April 2, 1998, by and among the Corporation, Joseph P. Connors, William Petmecky, Kip Gundry, and Troy Horton, shareholder and/or employees of or distributors for the Corporation, and the Purchaser (the "Purchase Agreement"), the Purchaser is purchasing substantially all of the assets of the Corporation and the business of the Corporation as a going concern.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to the 'Purchase Agreement', consistent with the contradicted text, which explicitly defines the 'Purchase Agreement' as the 'Asset Purchase Agreement of April 2, 1998'. The changed text replaces 'Purchase Agreement' with 'Asset Transfer Agreement'. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text specifically names the agreement as the 'Purchase Agreement,' not the 'Asset Transfer Agreement', leading to confusion about which agreement is being referenced. This misalignment causes uncertainty and violates the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.explanation: Changing 'COVENANTS' to 'CONDITIONS' in the section title. While conceptually similar, these terms have distinct legal meanings. 'Covenants' imply a promise within a contract, while 'Conditions' may refer to prerequisites or triggers for certain obligations. This shift creates ambiguity regarding the legal enforceability of the restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration for the covenants contained herein, the Purchaser has agreed to enter into the Asset Purchase Agreement to be executed in conjunction with this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'COVENANTS' to 'CONDITIONS' introduces ambiguity, as 'Covenants' imply a promise within a contract, while 'Conditions' may refer to prerequisites or triggers. The contradicted text explicitly refers to 'covenants contained herein' as consideration for the agreement. By changing 'covenants' to 'conditions,' the modified text creates a contradiction because the consideration mentioned in the contradicted text is now unclear, causing a contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology where the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 It is hereby agreed that none of the Undersigneds shall, directly, or indirectly, for itself or themselves, or through or on behalf of any other person or entity including, without limitation, family members, trusts, or other business or estate planning arrangements engage in the following: (a) at any time, divulge, transmit, or otherwise disclose or cause to be divulged, transmitted, or otherwise disclose, any business contacts, client, distributor, or customer lists, technology, know-how, traded secrets, marketing techniques, supplier contacts, contracts, or other confidential or proprietary information of the Corporation of whatever nature existing on or prior to the date hereof (provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (i) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record, (ii) it is generally known in the industry in which the Business is engaged, or (iii) the Undersigneds can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non-disclosure agreement);
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 It is hereby agreed that none of the Undersigneds shall, directly, or indirectly, for itself or themselves, or through or on behalf of any other person or entity including, without limitation, family members, trusts, or other business or estate planning arrangements engage in the following: (a) at any time, divulge, transmit, or otherwise disclose or cause to be divulged, transmitted, or otherwise disclose, any business contacts, client, Vendor, or customer lists, technology, know-how, traded secrets, marketing techniques, supplier contacts, contracts, or other confidential or proprietary information of the Corporation of whatever nature existing on or prior to the date hereof (provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (i) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record, (ii) it is generally known in the industry in which the Business is engaged, or (iii) the Undersigneds can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non-disclosure agreement);
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'distributor' to 'Vendor.' The term 'distributor' is established in previous sections, however 'Vendor' is introduced and is not clearly interchangeable with 'distributor.' If a 'Vendor' has less formal ties than a distributor, then divulging a list of Vendors would be permissible.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A. The Corporation has been engaged in the business of designing, developing, organizing, and managing golfing events as well as marketing its events through a network of independent distributors (the "Business").
perturbation.justification: The change from 'distributor' to 'Vendor' in Section 1.2(a), combined with the fact that Section A defines the 'Business' as marketing events through a network of independent distributors, creates a contradiction. The explanation states that 'Vendor' may have less formal ties than a distributor, which implies divulging a list of vendors may be permissible, whereas the original agreement was to protect the list of distributors. This contradicts the description of the Business's marketing strategy which relies on distributors, making it a strong in-text contradiction according to the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 In the event of a breach of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such breach may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such breach, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 In the event of a default of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such default may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such default, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the default.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'breach' to 'default' in the first line of section 4.1. While the terms are often used interchangeably, they have different legal connotations. 'Breach' typically refers to a violation of the terms of the agreement, while 'default' might refer to failure to perform a specific obligation. Here, it creates ambiguity regarding what actions trigger the liquidated damages clause. Especially since 'Breach' is used later in the same Section
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 In the event of a default of this Agreement by any either of the Undersigneds, the precise amount of damages that may be suffered by the Purchaser of reason of such default may be difficult to ascertain; accordingly, the parties hereby agree that, as liquidated damages (and not as a penalty) in respect of any such default, the Purchaser, in its sole discretion, may choose to demand the sum of $20,000 as the sole and exclusive damages for the default. The parties agree that the foregoing provision for liquidated damages, if chosen by Purchaser, constitutes a fair and reasonable estimate of the actual damages that might be suffered by reason of a breach of this Agreement by the Undersigneds.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'breach' to 'default' in section 4.1 introduces a contradiction. The contradicted text, which is also section 4.1, uses both 'default' and 'breach'. Specifically, it states that the liquidated damages are a fair estimate 'by reason of a breach of this Agreement'. By changing the first instance to 'default', but retaining 'breach' in the explanation of the liquidated damages, the contract now implies that liquidated damages are for both 'default' and 'breach', but only quantifies it to the fair damages of 'breach'. This misaligned terminology, as defined in the Misaligned Terminology perturbation, creates a direct conflict and ambiguity about when the liquidated damages apply and how they are estimated, which can lead to conflicting obligations and disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 5.9 Dispute Resolution. Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the ------------------ parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be submitted for mediation in Salt Lake City, Utah.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.9 Claim Resolution. Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the ------------------ parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be submitted for arbitration in Salt Lake City, Utah.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Dispute Resolution' to 'Claim Resolution' and 'mediation' to 'arbitration'. While all are valid ADR methods, they have different meaning and legal implications. 'Claim resolution' may narrow the scope of what is covered, implying it applies only to monetary claims and Arbitration means giving up your rights for a third party to resolve it, instead of mediation
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.9 Claim Resolution. Any controversy, claim, or dispute among the ------------------ parties hereto arising out of or related to this Agreement or the breach hereto, which cannot be settled amicably by the parties, shall be submitted for arbitration in Salt Lake City, Utah. In the event mediation is unsuccessful, the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of an appropriate court within Salt Lake County, State of Utah, to hear and decide any controversy, claim, or dispute hereunder. The prevailing party in any legal action shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, as determined by the trial court.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'Dispute Resolution' and 'mediation'. The changed text modifies this to 'Claim Resolution' and 'arbitration'. The contradicted text contains both the term 'Claim Resolution' and a clause stating that 'In the event mediation is unsuccessful, the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of an appropriate court', which directly conflicts with the changed text's replacement of mediation with arbitration. The modified text creates a contradiction by removing the initial step of mediation and the possibility of court jurisdiction after failed mediation, while the contradicted text anticipates and allows for mediation and subsequent court action.
